claims.claim: frontmatter code_ref stream_pacer.py:117 — SentenceStreamPacer tracks remaining_audio and gates next batch
claims.found: L117 audio_duration = self._audio_emitter.pushed_duration(); L131-133 remaining_audio = (audio_start_time + audio_duration - curr_time ...)
L131-133: remaining_audio = (audio_start_time + audio_duration - curr_time if audio_start_time > 0.0 else 0.0)

claims.claim: only sends next text batch when remaining audio falls below min_remaining_audio (default 5.0s)
claims.found: L20: def __init__(self, *, min_remaining_audio: float = 5.0, ...); L134-135: if first_sentence or (generation_stopped and remaining_audio <= self._options.min_remaining_audio)

claims.claim: Together: RTF = audio produced per second of processing; 10s audio in 5s = RTF 0.5. Want RTF < 1 to avoid buffering (together-talk L27)
claims.found: L27: '**Real-time factor (RTF):** audio produced per second of processing. 10s audio in 5s = RTF 0.5. **Want RTF < 1** to avoid buffering.'

claims.claim: LiveKit doesn't measure RTF but defends against RTF>1 with a remaining-audio watermark; mechanism is consumer-side gating
claims.found: Pacer computes remaining_audio from pushed audio duration and gates batch send on <= min_remaining_audio; no RTF constant exists in pacer (grep confirms). Consumer-side watermark mechanism accurately described.

summary: All 5 checkable assertions verified against ground truth. code_ref line 117 and the 117-135 window correctly show the remaining_audio formula and the min_remaining_audio=5.0 default gate (init at L20). The together-talk L27 quote is verbatim. The mechanism framing (LiveKit defends consumer-side without naming RTF) is accurate. PASS.
